The last known obstruction before degree 125 has fallen.

In 2022, Guccione, Guccione, Horruitiner and Valqui classified every possible degree pair below 125 for a hypothetical counterexample to the 2D Jacobian Conjecture. They eliminated all of them except one:

(deg P, deg Q) = (72,108), up to symmetry.

Today, working with GPT-5.6 Sol, we completed an exact characteristic-zero certificate replay excluding both Laurent/Newton systems produced by their published reduction.

For one configuration, we verified a unit-ideal identity:

1 = sum T_i R_i.

For the harder configuration, the system splits exhaustively into s = c and s = -c. On the first branch we verified:

h = sum T_i E_i,

so every common zero would require h = 0. But h = 0 is excluded by an exact unit-ideal certificate. An algebraic involution transports the argument to the second branch, whose specialization is independently certified as impossible.

The hard certificate is 89.1 MB. It was reconstructed from a fixed 1925 x 1925 rational system and checked against all 2010 scalar equations. An independent verifier replayed 13,410 number-field products - 335,250 scalar products.

No floating-point approximation. No finite-field obstruction used as the final argument. Only exact polynomial identities in characteristic zero.

Conditional on the correctness and exhaustiveness of the published reduction and its transcription, every hypothetical 2D counterexample must now satisfy:

max(deg P, deg Q) >= 125.

This is not a proof of JC2. It moves its verified computational frontier.

After the newly announced counterexample in dimension 3, the landscape is now:

n = 1: true
n = 2: open; any counterexample is forced to degree >= 125, conditionally as above
n >= 3: false

The previous mountain was (72,108). The next one begins at 125.

Paper and exact certificates attached. Independent audit welcome.

https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.14178

#JacobianConjecture #ComputerAlgebra #AIforMath #GPT56Sol #AlgebraicGeometry
